edge: Neural networks are trained on algorithmic tasks, which raises the question of whether they can rediscover known algorithms for those tasks.
Several recent studies have suggested that neural networks can rediscover known algorithms for a variety of tasks.
Neural networks can discover different algorithms based on small changes to hyperparameters and initializations, leading to a diversity of solutions for a fixed training set.
Some networks trained to perform modular addition implement the Clock algorithm, a previously described algorithm.
Other networks trained for modular addition implement the Pizza algorithm, a previously undescribed but comprehensible procedure.
Networks trained for modular addition can implement a variety of complex procedures, showcasing a surprising diversity of solutions for simple learning problems.
Understanding deep network models involves reverse-engineering their learned algorithms and representation schemes, which is a significant challenge across problem domains.
Recent studies have shown specific examples of models rediscovering algorithms.

The Clock algorithm is implemented to perform modular addition by specific neural network architectures, as shown by Nanda et al. This relationship shows a specific approach to learning modular addition.

The Pizza algorithm, a qualitatively different approach to modular arithmetic, is preferentially implemented by networks structurally similar to the ones trained by Nanda et al. This shows a variety in approaches to solving the same problem.

Models exhibit sharp algorithmic phase transitions between the Clock and Pizza algorithms as their width and attention strength vary, suggesting different approaches to solving modular arithmetic problems.
The transition between the Clock and Pizza algorithms is discussed in the context, highlighting their differences in width and attention strength.
The code for the Pizza algorithm is available at the provided GitHub link, allowing for further exploration and implementation.
The Clock and Pizza algorithms are illustrated in Figure 1, emphasizing the comparison between the two.
Empirical evidence is presented in Section 2, showing deviations from the Clock algorithm in models trained for modular addition.
In Section 3, an alternative Pizza algorithm is introduced to explain the deviations observed in models trained for modular addition.
Additional metrics are defined in Section 4 to distinguish between Clock and Pizza algorithms and detect phase transitions between them when architectural variations occur.
Phase transitions between the Pizza algorithm and other non-circular algorithms are discussed in Section 4.
The relationship between phase transitions and algorithm interpretability is explored in the context, emphasizing the importance of characterizing algorithmic phase spaces.
The networks take as input embeddings and predict a categorical output. Both embeddings and network parameters are learned.
Two different network architectures trained on the modular arithmetic task.
Model A is a one-layer ReLU transformer with constant attention.
Model B is a standard one-layer ReLU transformer.
As attention is not involved in Model A, it can be understood as a ReLU MLP.
After training both Model A and Model B, embeddings usually describe a circle in the plane spanned by the first two principal components of the embedding matrix.
Nanda et al. discovered a circuit that uses circular embeddings from Model A to implement an interpretable algorithm for modular arithmetic, which we call the Clock algorithm.
Clock algorithm can be applied to Model B as well.
The Clock algorithm is mentioned in the context as a concept.
The Clock algorithm contrasts with non-circular concepts in the context.
Learned embeddings may have gradient symmetry.
The presence of gradient symmetry does not require non-linearity in the context.
The clock algorithm described in the context implements a form of modular arithmetic where angles are added together and evaluated to determine the result.
Nanda et al. find that neural networks like Model B implement the Clock algorithm, which involves representing tokens as 2D vectors and adding their polar angles using trigonometric identities.
Tokens a and b are represented as 2D vectors in neural networks like Model B to implement the Clock algorithm.
The attention mechanism in the model variants can be leveraged to perform multiplication, which is crucial for the algorithm described.
Model A deviates from the Clock algorithm when the attention mechanism is absent.
Model A shows evidence of deviation from the Clock algorithm.
The gradients of Qabc lack permutation symmetry which is relevant for the Clock algorithm.
The gradients of output logits are computed with respect to the input embedding vectors.
Model B demonstrates asymmetry in general.
Model A exhibits gradient symmetry.
The output logit c is computed for the input (a, b) by taking gradients.
X and y axes represent the gradients for embeddings of the first and the second token.

Model A is described as performing modular arithmetic and an alternative solution is introduced, the Pizza algorithm.
The Pizza algorithm is proposed as a new solution for modular arithmetic.
The Pizza algorithm is a new approach for modular arithmetic that operates inside a circle instead of on the circumference, providing a different methodology for performing mathematical calculations.
The Pizza algorithm is distinguished from the Clock algorithm by operating inside the circle formed by embeddings, while the Clock algorithm operates on the circumference of the circle.
The Pizza algorithm is claimed to be the procedure implemented by Model A.3.1, suggesting a practical application of the algorithm in a specific model.
The Pizza algorithm operates inside the circle formed by embeddings, akin to how pepperoni are spread all over a pizza, giving a visual analogy to its operation.
In the Pizza algorithm, points lie on a line through the origin of a 2D plane, illustrating the basic idea behind the algorithm's operation.
A network can utilize the Pizza algorithm to determine which slice pair the average of two embedding vectors lies in, showcasing a practical application of the algorithm in network computations.
The Pizza algorithm divides points into two out of 2p mirrored 'pizza slices', allowing for a distinct categorization of data points based on their embeddings.

The key difference between the Pizza and Clock algorithms lies in the non-linear operations required. Clock requires multiplication of inputs, while Pizza requires only absolute value computation.
Both Pizza and Clock algorithms compute logits Qabc in Step 3, but they have different forms. Qabc(Pizza) has an extra multiplicative factor compared to Qabc(Clock).
The norm of the vector Eab depends on the difference between a and b, as mentioned in the context.
The norm of the vector depends on a - b.
Model A indeed exhibit a strong dependence on a - b.
Model A and Model B continue to behave in interpretable ways.
The algorithm replaces the embedding matrix E with rank-2 approximations.
For both Model A and Model B, embeddings form a circle in a two-dimensional subspace.
Model A's accuracy drops to zero at specific values of a - b, while Model B's accuracy is invariant in a - b.
Applying circle isolation to Model A yields a model with 32.8% overall accuracy.
Retaining the first six principal components yields an overall accuracy of 91.4% for Model B.
Model B achieves 100% accuracy when embeddings are truncated to the first six principal components.
When algorithm exhibits systematic errors on a subset of inputs, models can implement multiple algorithm variants in parallel to obtain more robust predictions.

Logits Qabc(Clock) is analyzed to not depend on a-b, but surprisingly dependent on a+b. This sensitivity is hypothesized due to noiseless implementation of Step 1 and Step 2 resulting in same-label samples collapsing at a common point after Step 2, with fluctuations occurring during Step 3 (classification) leading to common sum inputs producing the same logits.

The Achilles' heel of the Pizza algorithm is identified as antipodal pairs, where if two inputs (a, b) are antipodal, their middle point lies at the origin making it difficult to determine the correct 'pizza slice'.
Antipodal pairs have middle points that lie at the origin.
Antipodal pairs have different class labels.
Approximately antipodal pairs are more likely to be classified incorrectly.
An accompanied pizza and its accompanying pizza complement each other.
The difference between adjacent numbers on the circle is related to δ, δ1, and δ2 for accompanied and accompanying pizzas.
Pizzas #1/#2/#3 correspond to three different 'lottery tickets' at initialization.
Lottery tickets are different in the presence of weaknesses and the early stages of training require compensation for these weaknesses.
In the early stages of training, pizzas #1/#2/#3 are formed to compensate for the weaknesses (antipodal pairs) of pizzas #4/#5/#6.
In the presence of weight decay, the neural network gets pruned which leads to certain pizzas not significantly involved in prediction.
Accompanying pizzas #1/#2/#3 achieve 99.7% accuracy, while accompanying pizzas #4/#5/#6 can only achieve 16.7% accuracy.
Section 3 demonstrates a typical Clock (Model A) and a typical Pizza (Model B) and studies how architectures and hyperparameters govern the selection of these algorithmic 'phases'.
Section 4.1 proposes quantitative metrics that can distinguish between Pizza and Clock algorithms statistically.
Section 4.2 observes how metrics behave with different architectures and hyperparameters, showing sharp phase transitions.
Other algorithmic solutions to modular addition are discovered and explored in more detail in Appendix B.4.1.

Two metrics, gradient symmetricity, and distance irrelevance are introduced.
The concept of gradient symmetricity is defined in the context.
The cosine similarity is computed between the input-output group and the gradient vectors.
The gradient symmetricity of a network M with the embedding layer E is calculated.
The Pizza algorithm is mentioned to have symmetric gradients.
The Clock algorithm is referred to have asymmetric gradients.
Model A and Model B have different levels of gradient symmetricity.
Distance Irrelevance measures the dependence of correct logits on differences between two inputs.
The variance in the correct logit matrix depending on input differences is analyzed.

Distance irrelevance is defined for a network M with correct logit matrix L, where the standard deviation is calculated based on the logit values. It is a metric that quantifies the relevance of the distances in the network.
Model A in Section 3 has a distance irrelevance of 0.17, indicating how relevant the distances are in the context of the model.
Model B in Section 3 has a distance irrelevance of 0.85, suggesting a higher relevance of distances compared to Model A.
The Pizza algorithm typically has a distance irrelevance ranging from 0 to 0.4, indicating its dependency on the distances within the context.
The Clock algorithm typically has a distance irrelevance ranging from 0.4 to 1.4, showing a different dependency on distances compared to the Pizza algorithm.

In the context, when there are conflicting results, distance irrelevance is considered the decisive factor for the Pizza algorithm, while gradient symmetricity is used for ruling out the Clock algorithm.
Gradient symmetricity is used to rule out the Clock algorithm as it requires multiplying inputs, leading to asymmetric gradients.

Relationship between asymmetric gradients and distance irrelevance is indicated by Figure 6 where it is confirmed that the gradient symmetricity is almost always close to 1 at low distance irrelevance.
Figure 6 shows the relationship between distance irrelevance and gradient symmetricity over all the main experiments.
The relationship between gradient symmetricity and low distance irrelevance is established by Figure 6, where the symmetricity is close to 1 at low distance irrelevance.
There is a relationship between models and algorithms as models 'choose' whether to implement the Clock or Pizza algorithm. This investigation is done by interpolating between Model A and Model B.
The attention rate α is a hyperparameter that helps in the selection of models with or without attention. It controls the balance of attention versus linear layers in transformers.

The attention rate affects the attention matrix by modifying it to consist of a linear interpolation between the all-one matrix and the original attention. The rate α controls how much of the attention is kept.
The relationship between the transformer and attention rate is established through the modification of the attention matrix based on the attention rate α.
Experiments are performed on transformers, as mentioned in the context. The experiments involve different settings such as the width and attention rate for one-layer transformers.

One-layer transformers are mentioned with different configurations such as width 128 and attention rate uniformly sampled in [0, 1], log-uniformly sampled in [32, 512], and 2 to 4 layers with width 128. This indicates a relationship where transformers can have different configurations based on layer and parameters.
Pizza algorithm and Clock algorithm are mentioned as dominating algorithms with circular embeddings. This suggests a relationship between the choice of algorithm and the type of embeddings used.

Pizza algorithm and Clock algorithm are mentioned with different properties such as low gradient symmetricity and low distance irrelevance for the corresponding models. This indicates a relationship between the algorithm type and the model properties.
Dominance of linear layers determines whether the Pizza or the Clock algorithm is preferred. This suggests a relationship between the type of layers used in transformers and the choice of algorithm.

Two-dimensional phase change observed for attention rate and layer width, indicating a relationship between attention rate and model behavior. Additionally, the Clock algorithm dominates when the attention rate is higher than the phase change point, while the Pizza algorithm dominates when it's lower, suggesting a strong relationship between attention rate and algorithm choice.

Two-dimensional phase change observed for attention rate and layer width, indicating a relationship between width and model behavior. Additionally, for one-layer transformers, the Clock algorithm dominates when the attention rate is higher than the phase change point, and the Pizza algorithm dominates when it's lower, suggesting a strong relationship between width and algorithm choice.
The Pizza algorithm dominates when the attention rate is lower than the point.
At a high attention rate, the attention mechanism is more prominent in the network, giving rise to the clock algorithm.
The phase change point gets higher when the model width increases. When the model gets wider, the linear layers become more capable.
When the model gets wider, the attention mechanism receives less benefit compared to linear layers.
The linear layer therefore gets more prominence with a wider model.
Possibly hybrid algorithms between the Clock and the Pizza algorithms.

node_2: principal components acting as the pizza
edge: The continuous phase change suggests the existence of networks that lie between the Clock and the Pizza algorithms. This is achievable by having some principal components acting as the Clock and some principal components acting as the Pizza.
Logistic regression is used to classify the runs based on whether gradient symmetricity is greater than 98% and whether distance irrelevance is less than 0.6.
The existence of non-circular algorithms is mentioned in the context.
Circular algorithms (whose embeddings are circular) are compared with non-circular algorithms (whose embeddings do not form a circle when projected onto any plane) in neural networks.
Deeper networks are found to be more likely to form non-circular algorithms.
Non-circular networks are observed at low attention rates in the context.
The Pizza algorithm is mentioned to continue being observed with low distance irrelevance and high gradient symmetricity.

The emergence of some model behaviors results from a sequence of quantized circuit discovery steps.
Familiar algorithms arise in neural networks trained on specific algorithmic tasks.
In modular arithmetic, it has been shown that various algorithmic discoveries are prevalent in trained models, such as the Clock algorithm and Pizza algorithm.

Different algorithmic phases can be distinguished using interpretability techniques such as logit visualization, isolation of principal components in embedding space, and gradient-based measures of model symmetry.
Clock algorithm and Pizza algorithm are examples of different algorithms found to be prevalent in trained models.
The emergence of a Pizza or Clock algorithm depends on the relative strength of linear layers and attention outputs in neural networks.
Networks sometimes ensemble multiple copies of an algorithm in parallel.
The results offer new challenges for mechanistic interpretability, including finding, classifying, and interpreting unfamiliar algorithms in a systematic way.
Revealing algorithmic phase transitions in the space of model hyperparameters based on the algorithms implemented in trained networks.
Interpretability techniques can play a crucial role in understanding and interpreting unfamiliar algorithms in a systematic way.
The challenge is to disentangle multiple, parallel algorithm implementations in the presence of ensembling.
Qualitatively different model behaviors emerge across architectures and seeds.
Interpretability techniques can help in creating and improving safe AI systems.
There are attendant risks inherent in interpretability techniques, as they can be used to build more accurate systems.
Scaling techniques to more complex models used in real-world tasks requires significant additional work.
